slides.title: Welcome — how this game works
body: This game teaches Apex by having you TYPE code patterns instead of memorizing them. Code appears in light grey. You type each character. Correct keystrokes turn solid; wrong keys flash red and don't advance.
Why typing? Muscle memory is more durable than recall. After typing a pattern enough times, your fingers know it before your brain does.

slides.title: What is code, really?
body: Code is a list of instructions a computer runs top to bottom. Each instruction is called a STATEMENT. Most statements end with a semicolon ;
A program is built from VARIABLES (named buckets that hold values) and FUNCTIONS (named blocks of instructions you can re-run). Almost everything else is built on those two ideas.

vocabulary.def: One instruction in a program, usually one line, ending with a semicolon.
A named storage location that holds a value.
A reusable block of instructions identified by a name.

slides.title: Apex is typed, and case-insensitive
body: TYPED means: when you create a variable, you must declare what kind of value it holds — a number, text, a date, etc.
CASE-INSENSITIVE means: 'myAge' and 'MyAge' are the same to the compiler. But still pick one style and be consistent — other developers will care.

vocabulary.def: The language requires you to declare what kind of value each variable holds.

slides.title: Your very first line of code
slides.intro: Type this. We'll explain it after the line completes.
lineExplanations: System.debug() prints a message. 'System' is a class Salesforce gives you. 'debug' is a method on it. The text in single quotes is what you want printed. Semicolon ; ends the statement.

slides.title: Anatomy of a variable
body: Three parts in order: the TYPE, the NAME, the VALUE.
Example: Integer myAge = 30;  →  Integer is the type, myAge is the name, 30 is the value, ; ends it.

vocabulary.def: What KIND of value a variable holds — Integer, String, Boolean, etc.
The actual data stored — 30, 'hello', true, etc.

slides.title: camelCase vs PascalCase
body: Variables and methods use camelCase: lowercase first word, capitalize each next word — myAge, isLearning.
Types and class names use PascalCase: capitalize EVERY word — Integer, String, Account.

vocabulary.def: thisIsCamelCase — first word lowercase, each next word capitalized.
ThisIsPascalCase — every word capitalized, including the first.

slides.title: Three variables, three types
slides.intro: Type each line. Notice the same shape: TYPE name = value;
lineExplanations: Integer holds whole numbers. 'myAge' is the name. 30 is the value. The = assigns the value to the variable.
String holds text. Apex strings use SINGLE quotes — never double quotes (a common bug from other languages).
Boolean holds only two possible values: true or false. No quotes — true and false are language keywords.

slides.front:
What does this line do?

Integer myAge = 30;
slides.back: Declares a variable named myAge of type Integer, and assigns it the value 30.
slides.detail: Three parts: TYPE (Integer) · NAME (myAge) · VALUE (30). The = is the assignment operator.

slides.question: Which of these is the correct way to write a piece of text in Apex?
slides.explanation: Apex uses SINGLE quotes for strings. Double quotes are not valid for string literals in Apex. The type is also capitalized: String, not string.

slides.title: Curly braces { } group code
body: When you see { }, they're saying 'the stuff between us belongs together.' Used for class bodies, method bodies, loops, and if-blocks.
Indentation inside { } is for human readability — Apex itself ignores it. Other developers will care a lot. Use it consistently.

vocabulary.def: Code grouped inside { } that runs together as a unit.

slides.title: Your first if-statement
slides.intro: Conditionals choose which lines to run. Notice how { } groups the lines that run when the condition is true.
lineExplanations: Declare an Integer variable named 'score' with value 85.
if (condition) starts a conditional. The parentheses hold the test. The { opens the block that runs when the test is true.
This line runs ONLY if the test was true. The four spaces are for humans — Apex ignores them.
The closing } ends the if-block. Anything after this } runs regardless of the score.

slides.title: Parameter vs Argument
body: A PARAMETER is the placeholder a method declares — like 'Decimal speed' in 'drive(Decimal speed)'.
An ARGUMENT is the actual value you pass when calling — like 60 in 'drive(60)'. Same value, two perspectives.

vocabulary.def: A named slot a method declares — defined where the method is written.
The actual value you pass to a method when calling it.

slides.front: When you call drive(60), what is the role of '60'?
slides.detail: The parameter (named slot) is defined where the method is written. The argument (actual value) is supplied at the call site.

slides.title: Property vs Field
body: Both mean 'a piece of data attached to a thing.' In Apex CLASSES we usually say PROPERTY.
On Salesforce RECORDS (SObjects like Account, Contact) we say FIELD. ATTRIBUTE is a more generic word for either.

vocabulary.def: A named piece of data attached to a class instance.
A named piece of data on a Salesforce record (an SObject).

slides.title: Function vs Method
body: Technically the same: a named, reusable block of code.
In object-oriented languages like Apex, a function defined inside a class is called a METHOD. Apex code always lives inside classes, so you'll mostly hear 'method'.

vocabulary.def: A named, reusable block of code defined inside a class.

slides.title: Class vs Object
body: A CLASS is the blueprint — like the idea of a Car.
An OBJECT (also called an instance) is one actual thing built from the blueprint — your specific red Honda. 'Object' and 'instance' are interchangeable.

vocabulary.def: A blueprint that defines what data and methods objects of its kind have.
An instance built from a class. Has its own copies of the class's properties.

slides.front: What's the difference between a class and an object?
slides.back: A class is the BLUEPRINT. An object (instance) is one ACTUAL thing built from the blueprint.
slides.detail: Example: Automobile is the class. The specific red sedan you constructed in code is an object.

slides.title: Constructor
body: A special method that runs automatically when you create a new object from a class.
Its job is to set up the object's initial state — assigning starting values to its properties.

vocabulary.def: A special method that runs when an object is created, used to set initial state.

slides.title: Putting it together — a tiny method
slides.intro: A complete Apex class with one method. Type it; explanations tag each line. You'll see this exact shape over and over.
lineExplanations: 'public' = anyone can use this class. 'class Greeter' = we're defining a class named Greeter. The { opens the class body.
Method shape: public (anyone can call), static (belongs to the class), void (returns nothing), sayHello (name), (String name) (one parameter), { (opens the method body).
Print 'Hello, ' joined to whatever was passed in. The + operator joins (concatenates) two strings.
This } closes the method. The next } closes the class. Always match opens with closes.

slides.title: What's next
body: You just typed your first complete class with a method. Every Apex lesson from here builds on this same skeleton.
Click 'Finish lesson' to mark Lesson 0 done. Your progress saves automatically. Click 'Sync to GitHub' when you're ready to push it.
